title: Hello Universe
content:
🌌 Welcome to the UniverseThis is a test post to verify that our Glassmorphism theme is working correctly across all pages.

Testing H2 HeadingsIf you can read this heading clearly against the aurora background, the white text styling is working. The heading should have a subtle gradient effect (cyan to purple).
Testing H3 HeadingsThis is a third-level heading. It should also be clearly visible with good contrast.

Body Text TestThis paragraph tests the body font. We’re using Outfit as our interface font—a clean, modern sans-serif that’s easy to read on screens. No Times New Roman here!
Here’s some bold text and italic text to check styling. And here’s a link to the homepage to test link colors.

“The best code is no code at all.” — Jeff Atwood
This blockquote should have a glassmorphism background with a cyan left border.


Code Block TestHere’s a JavaScript code block to test syntax highlighting and contrast:
// Galaxy Ripple Effectfunction createRipple(x, y) {  const ripple = document.createElement('span');  ripple.className = 'click-ripple';  ripple.style.left = x + 'px';  ripple.style.top = y + 'px';  document.body.appendChild(ripple);    setTimeout(() => ripple.remove(), 500);}document.addEventListener('click', (e) => {  createRipple(e.clientX, e.clientY);});

And some inline code: const theme = 'cosmic-void';

List TestUnordered List
First item with white text
Second item to check spacing
Third item with a link

Ordered List
First numbered item
Second numbered item
Third numbered item


SummaryIf all the elements above are:

✅ Readable (white text on glass)
✅ Properly styled (Outfit font)
✅ Inside a glassmorphism container
✅ Consistent with the homepage cards

Then our theme is working correctly! 🎉

title: The Art of Residual Networks
content:
The Art of Residual NetworksIn the world of deep learning, Residual Networks (ResNets) revolutionized how we think about training very deep neural networks. But the concept of “residuals” isn’t limited to AI—it’s a powerful mental model for problem-solving in general.

What Are Residuals?
“Instead of learning the full transformation, learn only the difference between input and output.”— The ResNet Philosophy

The core insight is elegant: rather than asking a layer to learn a complex function $H(x)$ from scratch, we ask it to learn only the residual $F(x) = H(x) - x$. The final output becomes $F(x) + x$.
This same principle applies to debugging CSS. Don’t rewrite everything—find the residual correction.

Applying Residual Thinking to CodeHere’s a simple Python example of a residual block:
import torchimport torch.nn as nnclass ResidualBlock(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, channels):        super().__init__()        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1)        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1)        self.relu = nn.ReLU()        def forward(self, x):        residual = x  # Save the input        out = self.relu(self.conv1(x))        out = self.conv2(out)        out += residual  # Add the skip connection        return self.relu(out)

The magic is in line out += residual—the skip connection that makes deep networks trainable.

Key TakeawaysHere’s what we can learn from ResNets:

Incremental improvement beats complete rewrites
Skip connections help information flow freely
Identity mappings preserve what already works
Depth becomes possible when gradients can flow


The CSS AnalogyWhen debugging a stylesheet, think like a ResNet:

Don’t delete the existing styles (that’s your identity mapping)
Append corrections that target specific issues
Use specificity to override only what’s broken
Test incrementally after each residual fix


“The best code change is the smallest one that solves the problem.”


ConclusionWhether you’re training neural networks or styling web pages, the residual approach keeps you grounded. Start with what works, identify what’s broken, and apply the minimum correction needed.
Next time you face a complex bug, ask yourself: “What’s the residual?”

title: Your Post Title Here

IntroductionWrite your opening paragraph here. This text appears in the preview card on the homepage.


Everything below this line is hidden from the homepage preview and only visible when the reader clicks into the full article.

Section HeaderRegular paragraph text goes here. You can use bold, italic, and inline code.
SubsectionHere’s how to add a blockquote:

“The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now.”— Chinese Proverb


Code Exampledef hello_cosmos():    """A simple function to greet the universe."""    stars = ["Polaris", "Sirius", "Vega"]    for star in stars:        print(f"Hello, {star}!")    return "🌌"hello_cosmos()

title: Welcome to the Cosmic Void
content:
🌌 Welcome, Traveler!You’ve entered the Cosmic Void — a digital space where code meets the stars.

“The cosmos is within us. We are made of star-stuff.” — Carl Sagan

✨ What You’ll Find Here
🚀 Technology — Exploring cutting-edge tech
💻 Code — Tutorials and programming wisdom
🔮 Future — Thoughts on where technology is taking us

🎆 Try the Effects!Move your mouse — Watch Jupiter orbit with its moons!Click anywhere — See planets explode outward!

The future isn’t written yet. Let’s code it together. 🌌
